skill.name: azure-monitor-ingestion-py
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure Monitor Ingestion SDK for Python to send custom logs to Azure Monitor Log Analytics with Data Collection Rules.
skill.summary: Send Python custom logs to Azure Monitor Log Analytics through the Logs Ingestion API.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static command-execution, weak-crypto, hidden-file, reconnaissance, and obfuscation heuristic alerts are false positives from Markdown examples, inline code formatting, Azure DCR terminology, and Azure endpoint strings. The real residual risks are normal for this skill: it documents outbound log ingestion to Azure Monitor, reads Azure configuration from environment variables, shows a local JSON file read, and includes an example that silently ignores upload errors.

low_findings.title: Outbound Azure Monitor Log Uploads
low_findings.description: The skill teaches users to upload custom logs to Azure Monitor. This is expected behavior, but logs may contain sensitive data if users send unfiltered application or system records.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload calls and Azure ingestion endpoints are explicit in the documentation. The risk is limited because the destination is the intended Azure Monitor service, not an unexplained third-party endpoint.

low_findings.title: Ambient Azure Credential Use
low_findings.description: Examples use DefaultAzureCredential and environment-provided Azure settings. This is standard Azure SDK usage, but users should review which identity is active before sending logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code examples directly instantiate DefaultAzureCredential and read Azure settings from os.environ. No evidence shows credential theft or disclosure beyond normal SDK authentication.

low_findings.title: Silent Upload Failure Handling
low_findings.description: The ignore_errors example suppresses upload failures. This can hide ingestion gaps and make monitoring data incomplete if copied into production code without changes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly defines an error callback that passes without reporting failures. This is a clear reliability and auditability concern, although it is not malicious.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives Reviewed
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution findings are Markdown code fences and inline code examples, and the weak-crypto findings are Azure DCR terminology. No prompt injection, obfuscation, or malicious execution path was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms the flagged locations are documentation formatting, Azure acronyms, tables, or sample commands. There are no executable repository scripts or instructions to bypass security review.

content.user_title: Send Azure Monitor Logs from Python
content.value_statement: Teams need reliable examples for sending custom Python logs to Azure Monitor. This skill provides focused SDK patterns for authentication, DCR streams, uploads, async usage, and error handling.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install azure-monitor-ingestion and azure-identity for Python projects.
Explains required Azure Monitor resources, including DCE, DCR, streams, and custom tables.
Provides synchronous and asynchronous LogsIngestionClient upload patterns.
Demonstrates reading DCE, DCR, and stream settings from environment variables.
Covers partial failure callbacks and retrying failed log records.
Includes sovereign cloud endpoint and credential scope examples.
limitations: Does not create Azure resources or DCR schemas for the user.
Does not validate log payloads against a real Log Analytics table.
Does not provide complete production security controls for sensitive log data.
Assumes users already have Azure permissions and workspace configuration.

use_cases.title: Add Custom Log Ingestion
use_cases.description: Implement Python code that sends application events to a Log Analytics custom table through a configured DCR stream.

use_cases.title: Build Operational Telemetry Pipelines
use_cases.description: Adapt sync or async ingestion examples for higher-volume service telemetry and monitor partial upload failures.

use_cases.title: Prepare Azure Monitor Examples
use_cases.description: Create concise documentation for DCE endpoints, DCR identifiers, stream names, and required environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Upload Example
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to write a simple Python example that uploads three custom log records to Azure Monitor Log Analytics with LogsIngestionClient.

prompt_templates.title: Add Environment Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show how my Python app should read AZURE_DCE_ENDPOINT, AZURE_DCR_RULE_ID, and AZURE_DCR_STREAM_NAME before uploading logs.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Partial Failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an on_error callback for Azure Monitor log ingestion that records failed logs and retries them safely.

prompt_templates.title: Design Async Log Ingestion
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline an async Azure Monitor ingestion flow for a Python service that sends batches to a DCR stream.

output_examples.input: Request: Explain the required Azure Monitor settings for Python log ingestion.
output_examples.output: A concise checklist covering the Data Collection Endpoint URL, Data Collection Rule immutable ID, stream name, custom table, and Azure identity requirements.

output_examples.input: Request: Improve error handling for custom log uploads.
output_examples.output: A recommended approach that captures partial failures, records failed log entries, retries them once, and avoids silently ignoring ingestion problems.

output_examples.input: Request: Compare sync and async clients for this SDK.
output_examples.output: A short decision guide that suggests the sync client for small scripts and the async client for higher-throughput service ingestion.

best_practices: Filter sensitive data before uploading logs to Azure Monitor.
Use DefaultAzureCredential only after confirming the active Azure identity and permissions.
Handle partial failures and verify ingestion results in Log Analytics.
anti_patterns: Do not copy the silent ignore_errors callback into production code.
Do not hardcode real DCE endpoints, rule IDs, or stream names in shared examples.
Do not send logs that do not match the DCR schema and custom table columns.

faq.question: What Azure SDK package does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers the azure-monitor-ingestion package and the LogsIngestionClient for Python.

faq.question: Does this skill create a Data Collection Rule?
faq.answer: No. It assumes the DCR, DCE, stream, and destination table already exist.

faq.question: Can it help with asynchronous uploads?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes the async LogsIngestionClient pattern for higher-throughput ingestion workflows.

faq.question: Does it require Azure credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples use DefaultAzureCredential, so the runtime environment needs a valid Azure identity.

faq.question: Is this safe for sensitive logs?
faq.answer: Only after filtering. Users should remove secrets, personal data, and regulated information before uploading logs.

faq.question: What should I verify before production use?
faq.answer: Verify the DCR schema, stream name, active Azure identity, error handling, and Log Analytics ingestion results.
